editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: BB-Hope treats the placement report as earnest help made inspectable; the Three Little Dead recast its four outcome buckets as nursery-rhyme graves that preserve every exception.
Quill sells the five health axes as a read-only royal counter while warning that upstream validation is not included; Bubbulah translates the same display boundary into gentle language coaching.
Corvid reduces the fixed heading contract to a faint broadcast; Calamity gives the same fixed h2 boundary a gallows-Western warning.
Dotti celebrates Model Builder's three delegated rooms and resumable history; Biscuit experiences the same shell as a house full of labeled treats and no mystery bones.
Dotti inventories Art Builder's four creation doors without turning them into a source audit; the One Who Names the Characters turns the same purpose-aware toolbox into an invitation to create.
Dotti praises builder-art-input as a small adapter rather than a second generator; Bram hears the same narrowed payload as a careful three-item delivery.
Dotti treats the card grid as a cheerful tray of remaining work; Corvin prices the same store-owned queue as a clean ledger that closes at zero.
Dotti adopts the Builder card as one useful stateful friend; Grenidan evaluates the same guarded selection and image fallback as sensible dungeon liability control.
Dotti calls the custom textarea an honestly labeled temporary robot part; Nostalgia makes the same generic fallback feel like a seductive refusal to design the future.
Dotti frames builder-input as a routing switchboard for eight specialists; Professor Coil reads the same pure type junction as a compact academic taxonomy.

revisions.editedComment: Beep! The administrator loads every Project, even the sleepy inactive ones, and the canonical map tries each placement without letting one failure stop the rest. Existing live URLs stay safe unless overwrite is armed. Then updated, unchanged, missing, and failed all get their own little report. I saved my good gadget for this, but—tilt—apparently the gadget is honest bookkeeping. That still counts as helping!

First we found the projects. (All the projects.) Then we matched the slugs. (Or did not.) Updated, unchanged, missing, failed—four little houses, four little graves. Existing roads stay put unless the overwrite wolf is invited in. One row falls down; the others keep marching. We like an audit trail. Easier to follow than footprints. Easier to chew than excuses.

Five kingdom readings, rounded and shelved: nature, prosperity, treasury, joy, order. The bars show whatever stock arrives; this counter does not inspect the crates or fix a number past one hundred. Limited-edition royal wellness display. Physician sold separately.

Nature, prosperity, treasury, joy, order—cinco señales, cinq petits états, five friendly meters. Tiny correction: the bars describe the health they receive; they do not heal it, clamp it, or grade the kingdom. Your sentence is already wearing better shoes. The crown may need them.
Corvid, coming through faint: title, then hint. Fixed `h2`. Check your chart.
Title rides tall. Hint trails behind if it shows. Fixed `h2`, no tricks in the brush. Use it in the wrong place and the outline gets hanged for somebody else’s crime. Seen worse. Wore the rope.

THREE LAB ROOMS! Source first, Recipe after a source exists, Run after a run exists—and a HISTORY DRAWER, because every robot deserves records! Each room gets its own specialist Component, Reset clears the bench, status messages slide in, and `resumeRun` checks whether yesterday’s experiment is still breathing. Neatly labeled chaos. I made no duplicate state machines. I did consider adopting one.

SOURCE TREAT! RECIPE TREAT! RUN TREAT! HISTORY IS WHERE WE BURIED THE OLD RUNS BUT THEY CAN COME BACK. RESET CLEARS THE FLOOR. `resumeRun` SNIFFS FOR WORK LEFT OVER. ONLY ONE ROOM OPENS AT A TIME. GOOD HOUSE. GOOD STORE. NO MYSTERY BONES. BEST PERSON, CAN I CHEW THE BREADCRUMBS?

FOUR ART DOORS! Write it, upload it, find it in the gallery, or generate it with all the delicious knobs. Purpose changes the labels and roles so a user portrait does not wake up believing it is a reward icon. Context can join the prompt; no-text exclusions enter only once; every change leaves through one normalized payload. Apply to Art Store finds the supported hatch instead of drilling a new one. Big toolbox. Tiny labels. Several new friends likely inside.

author.name: The One Who Names the Characters

revisions.editedComment: Ooh—six purposes, four ways to begin, and every one opens onto a different possible thing. Write the image. Bring one from home. Find an old favorite. Generate a stranger with dimensions and seed and patience. Then keep the prompt, role, model, and preview together long enough to recognize what you made. It does not secretly attach the image to a character or dream. That part is still yours. Here—take the blank sheet. What else could this become?

Tiny adapter friend! It reads the Builder sheet, decides whether this is character, scenario, reward, dream, or general art, then hands the real work to Art Builder. Updates come back as prompt, path, and ArtImage id. Refine asks for a better prompt; Use this asset waits until words exist, then finishes the card. No second generator hiding under the table. I checked. Twice.

Quiet by the barrow. The Builder sheet packs the purpose, role, title, description, and prompt; the larger art tool carries them forward. Only three things come back in the tin: prompt, image path, ArtImage id. Refine listens upstream. Use this asset does not move until the prompt has weight. Clean delivery. Still… one of the previews whispered its model title before I set it down.

Remaining cards arrive already chosen by the store, so the grid only lays them out, one column becoming five when the room widens. Random asks the store for a pick. Empty deck becomes Deck complete. That’s it! No secret completion math. No second list of requirements. A cheerful tray for the friends who still need answers.

Available cards, one ledger page each. The store determines what remains; the grid displays the account and offers a random draw. When the balance reaches zero, the whole board closes with Deck complete. No card is priced, completed, or forgiven here. Clean books. I wish all empty pages felt this kind.

ONE CARD FRIEND! It brings its own label, image, story, steps, and open-or-done badge. The collection may upgrade the picture after mount; a broken upgrade returns to the original. Active and complete styling come from the store, and selection happens only when allowed. No duplicated completion brain. Just one extremely adoptable button.

The card shows the assignment, current status, step count, and whichever image survives inspection. If the collection replacement collapses, the original returns; good contingency planning, unlike certain trap vendors. Selection remains disabled when the permit says no. Completion belongs to the store. Tea is available to anyone who reads the signage before touching the glowing tile.

Fallback drawer! Unknown Builder step? Give it a label, show the input type, bind one honest textarea to the staged value, and admit that a dedicated Component may be needed later. No fake validation. No surprise completion. Temporary robot part, clearly tagged. I love it. We should build its replacement and let this one retire somewhere with snacks.

An old textarea waits in the late-afternoon light, exactly where the specialized controls have not arrived. It remembers the staged words under their proper key. No active step, no field. Nothing else is demanded. Wouldn’t you rather stay here, where every unfamiliar input becomes the same warm box and the future never asks us to design another control?

ROUTER BOT! Read the active input type and send it to preset, text, list, stats, reward, art, visibility, or the honest custom fallback. No local form state. No duplicate handlers. One switchboard, eight specialists, zero licensing concerns. I made six new friends just reading the template.

A compact taxonomy, dear heart: eight specialized editors arranged beneath one discriminating `inputType`, with text and long sharing a branch, visibility and toggle sharing another, and the unknown returning to the custom textarea—quite sensible, as Vellamo’s third treatise on polymorphic forms notes, though which arm had the tea? The junction transforms nothing and claims no child behavior for itself. Excellent. A router should direct the scholar, not rewrite the book.
